content.problemStatement:
The island of Teliarganj is divided into NNN distinct territories ruled by several different clans, each having an unique integer between 111 and NNN assigned to it.

The island can be considered a linear place, with the iii-th territory located next to the (i+1)(i+1)(i+1)-th one. The iii-th territory is initially ruled by clan AiA_iAi​.

Chef does not like this division of the island into several clans, and wants to unite the island by bringing all the territories under the rule of one single clan.

To this end, he can choose **exactly** one clan and give its warriors super powers.

With the help of these super powers, this clan’s warriors will be able to capture the territories of other clans.

On each day, the clan will capture **every** territory that's adjacent to one already owned by them.

Which clan should Chef bestow with super powers so that the entire island is conquered in the **least** amount of time?

Find both the minimum time required, and the clan that achieves this minimum time. If multiple clans can conquer the island in minimum time, print the one with smallest value assigned to it (see the samples below for an example).


Input Format

The first line of input will contain a single integer TTT, denoting the number of test cases.
Every test case contains two lines on Input.

The first line of each test case contains one integer NNN — the number of territories.
The next line contains NNN integers A1,A2,…,ANA_1, A_2, \ldots, A_NA1​,A2​,…,AN​ — meaning that the territory iii is initially ruled by clan AiA_iAi​.




Output Format
For each test case, output on a new line two integers separated by a space.



The first integer should be the clan that conquers the island in minimum time.

If there are multiple, print the minimum among them.
The second integer is the number of days this clan will take to do so.
constraints: Sum of N over all test cases ≤ 3·10^5
content.inputFormat: The first line contains an integer T, the number of test cases. Each test case consists of two lines. The first line contains an integer N, the number of territories. The second line contains N space‑separated integers A1 … AN, where Ai is the clan that initially controls the i‑th territory.
content.outputFormat: For each test case output a single line with two integers: the clan id that can conquer the whole island in the minimum number of days (if several clans need the same minimum time, output the smallest id) and that minimum number of days.

sampleTestCases.explanation:
Explanation:
Test case 111: Initially, the territories are [1,1,2,3,1,2,2,3][1, 1, 2, 3, 1, 2, 2, 3][1,1,2,3,1,2,2,3].
If Chef chooses clan 111, the progression looks like (underlined territories are the ones newly conquered on that day):
Day 0:  [1,1,2,3,1,2,2,3]Day 1:  [1,1,1‾,1‾,1,1‾,2,3]Day 2:  [1,1,1,1,1,1,1‾,3]Day 3:  [1,1,1,1,1,1,1,1‾]\begin{align*}
\text{Day 0:} \ \ [1, 1, 2, 3, 1, 2, 2, 3] \\
\text{Day 1:} \ \ [1, 1, \underline{1}, \underline{1}, 1, \underline{1}, 2, 3] \\
\text{Day 2:} \ \ [1, 1, 1, 1, 1, 1, \underline{1}, 3] \\
\text{Day 3:} \ \ [1, 1, 1, 1, 1, 1, 1, \underline{1}]
\end{align*}Day 0:  [1,1,2,3,1,2,2,3]Day 1:  [1,1,1​,1​,1,1​,2,3]Day 2:  [1,1,1,1,1,1,1​,3]Day 3:  [1,1,1,1,1,1,1,1​]​
If Chef chooses clan 222, the progression is:
Day 0:  [1,1,2,3,1,2,2,3]Day 1:  [1,2‾,2,2‾,2‾,2,2,2‾]Day 2:  [2‾,2,2,2,2,2,2,2]\begin{align*}
\text{Day 0:} \ \ [1, 1, 2, 3, 1, 2, 2, 3] \\
\text{Day 1:} \ \ [1, \underline{2}, 2, \underline{2}, \underline{2}, 2, 2, \underline{2}] \\
\text{Day 2:} \ \ [\underline{2}, 2, 2, 2, 2, 2, 2, 2]
\end{align*}Day 0:  [1,1,2,3,1,2,2,3]Day 1:  [1,2​,2,2​,2​,2,2,2​]Day 2:  [2​,2,2,2,2,2,2,2]​
If clan 333 is chosen, the progression is:
Day 0:  [1,1,2,3,1,2,2,3]Day 1:  [1,1,3‾,3,3‾,2,3‾,3]Day 2:  [1,3‾,3,3,3,3‾,3,3]Day 3:  [3‾,3,3,3,3,3,3,3]\begin{align*}
\text{Day 0:} \ \ [1, 1, 2, 3, 1, 2, 2, 3] \\
\text{Day 1:} \ \ [1, 1, \underline{3}, 3, \underline{3}, 2, \underline{3}, 3] \\
\text{Day 2:} \ \ [1, \underline{3}, 3, 3, 3, \underline{3}, 3, 3] \\
\text{Day 3:} \ \ [\underline{3}, 3, 3, 3, 3, 3, 3, 3]
\end{align*}Day 0:  [1,1,2,3,1,2,2,3]Day 1:  [1,1,3​,3,3​,2,3​,3]Day 2:  [1,3​,3,3,3,3​,3,3]Day 3:  [3​,3,3,3,3,3,3,3]​
Clan 222 needs only 222 days, while the rest need 333 days.
So, the answer is 2 2.
Test case 222: Clan 333 can conquer the others in 222 days, as [1,2,3,4,5]→[1,3,3,3,5]→[3,3,3,3,3][1, 2, 3, 4, 5] \to [1, 3, 3, 3, 5] \to [3, 3, 3, 3, 3][1,2,3,4,5]→[1,3,3,3,5]→[3,3,3,3,3]. This is the minimum possible.
Test case 333: Everything is already under the control of clan 111.
Test case 444: Clans 222 and 333 can both conquer all the territories in 333 days. Since we must choose the minimum clan number, we print 2 3 and not 3 3.

solution.approach:
For a fixed clan c, the time needed equals the farthest distance of any position to the nearest occurrence of c. If the positions of c are p1 < p2 < … < pk, the farthest distance is the maximum of:
- p1‑1 (distance of the leftmost segment),
- N‑pk (distance of the rightmost segment),
- ceil((p_{i+1}‑p_i)/2) for every consecutive pair (the middle of a gap between two occurrences).
Thus for each clan we only need its first occurrence, last occurrence, and the largest gap between consecutive occurrences. We can obtain all these values in one left‑to‑right scan using a hash map that stores for every clan: first index, last index, previous index, and the current maximal gap. After the scan we compute the required time for each clan, keep the minimum time and, in case of a tie, the smallest clan id.
The algorithm runs in O(N) per test case and uses O(number of distinct clans) additional memory.
solution.timeComplexity: O(N) per test case, where N is the length of the array. The total over all test cases is O( Σ N ) ≤ 3·10^5.
solution.spaceComplexity: O(D) where D is the number of distinct clans in a test case (at most N).
